pattern: Detect whether the digit-square-sum sequence reaches 1 or enters a cycle; a hash set of seen values (or Floyd's cycle detection) identifies the cycle in O(log n) space.
hints: Repeatedly replace a number with the sum of the squares of its digits; if you ever reach 1 the number is happy, but if you revisit a value you're stuck in a cycle and it never will be.
A hash set of every value you have produced so far lets you detect a cycle in one pass: stop when the value is 1 (happy) or when it is already in the set (not happy).

Alternatively, treat the sequence as a linked list and apply Floyd's slow/fast pointer trick — the fast pointer advances two steps at a time; if it catches the slow pointer before hitting 1, a cycle exists.

edge_inputs.description: n=19 — becomes happy after a few steps
n=2 — enters the standard unhappy cycle quickly
n=7 — happy via a different path
n=2147483647 — near INT_MAX, must not loop or overflow
n=89 — well-known unhappy number that cycles through many values before repeating

stress.description: random integers across the full positive range
stress.note: exercises both happy and unhappy numbers at large magnitudes
